Jesus enters His final hours before the cross, and instead of terror, He prays—not for Himself, but for those the Father has given Him. This is not the prayer of someone facing defeat. Christ lifts His eyes to heaven with the authority of the eternal Son, asking the Father to glorify Him so that He may glorify the Father in return. He has already accomplished the work He was sent to do. What remains is not struggle, but the fulfillment of glory that belonged to Him before the world existed. In these moments, Jesus reveals something staggering: His entire mission has been about making the Father known to His disciples, and now He entrusts them to the Father's protection. The work of salvation is not uncertain or incomplete. It is accomplished. What follows is intercession—the risen Christ praying for those who will carry His name into a hostile world.

Notice what Jesus says eternal life actually is. Not endless existence, but knowledge—intimate, personal knowledge of the only true God and of Jesus Christ whom He sent. This is why Jesus spent three years with His disciples, not just performing miracles or teaching doctrine, but revealing the Father's character through His own words and presence. His disciples have kept the Father's word, received the words Jesus gave them, and come to certainty that He came from the Father. They are not many; they are those given to Him out of the world. Yet Jesus does not ask the Father to remove them from the world's hatred. Instead, He asks that they be protected within it, sanctified by truth, sent as He was sent. The world will hate them because they belong to Christ, not because they have failed. This is the cost of following Him, and it is non-negotiable.

What makes this prayer extraordinary is that Jesus does not pray only for the eleven. He prays for all who will believe through their message—which means He prays for you, across centuries, as if you were standing in that room. His intercession extends to perfect unity among all believers, a unity so profound that it reflects the union between Father and Son. He does not pray for comfort or ease, but for oneness, for sanctification, for the world to see in His people the love the Father has for Him. He prays that His joy would be fulfilled in them, even as they face opposition. This is the paradox of the Christian life: deep joy amid persecution, perfect love amid rejection, unity amid division. Jesus then makes the most audacious claim: He has given His disciples the glory the Father gave Him. They will bear His reflected glory into the world. On the night before His death, Jesus is not diminished. He is giving away His own glory to those who trust Him.

The prayer ends with Jesus's commitment to continue making the Father's name known. The work of revelation does not end at the cross—it continues through His Church, through the Spirit, until the end of the age. And the purpose is stunning: that the love the Father has for the Son would dwell in His disciples, and that Christ Himself would remain in them. This is not metaphorical comfort. This is the permanent indwelling of the risen Christ through the Holy Spirit, the presence of God among His people. As you read these words, you are reading the intercession of the ascended Christ on your behalf. He knows your name. He has prayed for your faith, your sanctification, your unity with His body, your witness to the world. The question is not whether He prays for you. The question is whether you will accept that you have been claimed by Him, protected by His name, and sent into the world as He was sent.
